Picante de Pollo

2 pounds (1 kg) of chicken breast

1⁄2 cup (120 ml) vegetable oil

4 cloves garlic, finely chopped

1 onion, finely chopped

3 pounds (1.3 kg) of tomatoes, peeled, seeded and chopped

1 teaspoon (5 ml) pepper

1⁄2 teaspoon (2.5 ml) hot pepper

1 teaspoon (5 ml) salt

Chili powder

1 can (15 ounces; 420 g) of pinto beans

1 cup (250 ml) of water

Salt and pepper to taste

In a large skillet, heat the oil. Add the garlic and onion, and sauté for 4 to 5 minutes. Add the tomatoes and seasonings. Simmer until the tomatoes are well cooked and have reduced in size.

Serve with white rice and a garden salad.

Human: What does the phrase "SOS" stand for? How was it created?

Assistant: It’s the International Morse Code emergency signal for “help.” The symbol of three dots, two dashes, and three dots was originally “S” or “Save Our Ship,” but the modern use is just the pattern. It’s still used in some maritime contexts for navigation and maritime signaling.  This is the story behind it.  Basically, a guy named Herbert O. Yardley was trying to invent a radio telegraph that could transmit Morse code without the use of a “sounder,” which was a telegraph key that would produce a tone and the typist would then transmit Morse code by copying that tone into the Morse key.  To produce the Morse code without the sounder, Yardley tried drawing dots and dashes on a “scrambled” typewriter, where the keys were all in random locations.  But this wasn’t as fast as typing by sound, so he taped the typewriter to the table and pounded out the dots and dashes with a hammer instead.  He drew this up on a sheet of paper, and realized that the set of dots and dashes made a symbol.

Yardley took his “SOS” drawings to Western Union, but the manager and his engineers were not interested.  Instead, they began using the SOS letters in their telegraph messages, for both emergency and test purposes.  

Later, the SOS signal was first used in a distress situation.  On November 7, 1898, the passenger steamer SS Wampanoag in the Atlantic Ocean sent out the call, “Come to the assistance of the SS Wampanoag, barque S.S. Abarenda, barque S.S. Wyoming, and sailing vessel Baltic.  Come to our relief.”  This came from a distress call sent out by the captain, C.D. Wadleigh, from the sinking SS Wampanoag.

Then later, in 1906, the SOS signal was standardized and became international.

“SOS” became the first Morse code word for “help”.
It’s also sometimes said that these letters were chosen because each begins with a consonant and ends with a vowel, to avoid confusion of which letter was being represented by the signal.

Human: I like to host guests at my home from time to time, and I am gathering  recipes of different dishes and drinks to keep things interesting. I am interested in trying some Belgium dishes. Can you give me a recipe for Carbonnade Flamande?

Assistant: You are in luck! Here is a recipe I found online:

Carbonnade flamande is a Belgian dish consisting of beef, chicken or beef and bacon in a brown sauce.  While it is usually made with beef, chicken, bacon and onions, there are also versions with fish and shellfish, such as shellfish in white wine sauce. Carbonnade is most commonly eaten with a variety of sauces (aka sauce rosette) and frites, but it is also eaten on its own. Carbonnade can be made with red or white wine and a number of spices, depending on the cook.  In Flanders, the region that surrounds Brussels in Belgium, the dish is often made with beer and brandy.

I found this recipe:

Ingredients

1 kilogram rump steak (beef)

150 grams rashers bacon

500 grams potatoes (peeled)

1 medium onion

1 garlic clove

6 peppercorns

1 bay leaf

1 tsp parsley

4 tbsp flour

1⁄2 liter red wine

1⁄2 liter beef stock (bouillon)

3 tbsp tomato puree

3 tbsp Worcestershire sauce

A dash of lemon juice

How To

Cook the bacon until crispy. Drain on paper towels and set aside.

Cut the meat into pieces. Brown the meat on all sides in a heavy pan. Remove and set aside.

Remove all but 1 tbsp of the bacon fat from the pan and add the onions, garlic, peppercorns, bay leaf, parsley and flour. Cook on low heat for about 5 minutes.

Add the wine, stock, tomato puree, Worcestershire sauce and lemon juice. Simmer for about 5 minutes.

Add the meat and bacon and cook over a low heat for about 30 minutes.

Mash the potatoes and add to the pan. Cook for about 10 more minutes.

Remove from the heat and serve with fries and a nice saucy sauce (either mayonnaise, homemade aioli, or rosette).

In a skillet, melt about a tablespoon of butter.  Place the patties in the skillet and place in the oven for about 5 minutes.  Turn over.  Cover.  Let cook another 5 minutes. For example, you want to use “greens” for the first stage of the compost pile and “browns” for the second stage. So basically, you’ll want to layer green food scraps from things like lettuce, cauliflower, green apples, etc. in the first layer of your compost pile, then cover it with a layer of brown stuff, like dryer lint or dry leaves.  When you do this, a soil microbe called a “predatory mite” will make the first stage of the compost pile grow. This “predatory mite” feeds on the life in the soil and is the start of the decomposition of the food waste. In order for your compost to turn into actual “soil” with all the nutrients in it that you want, you need to get to a second stage of composting with the “browns” where bacteria, fungi, and other microbes are present in the soil.

The first human brain transplant was performed by neurosurgeon Robert White in 1962. White was the first to record the survival of an animal brain transplant, and the first to carry out this type of surgery on a human in a living person (1963). Under that principle, it is legal to defend one’s property from intruders, even if the intruders are unarmed.  This makes sense, because property is an investment, and if you own it you should be able to preserve it from criminals.  

The Stand Your Ground law extends that principle to defense of yourself as well, though there are some restrictions on when you can use deadly force.  Specifically, it should only be used in “life-threatening situations” that happen in public.  And in some cases, you should avoid using deadly force even if it means you risk being harmed yourself. 2 1/4 cups all-purpose flour

1 cup whole milk

1 tablespoon sugar

3 egg yolks

3 egg whites

4 cloves garlic, peeled and minced

1/4 teaspoon salt

3 tablespoons vegetable oil, plus more for the bowl

water

cornstarch

Kosher salt

Black pepper

Minced garlic (for garnish)

Directions:

Combine all-purpose flour and milk in a bowl and stir with a wooden spoon until well blended.

Beat egg yolks, sugar, egg whites, 3 tablespoons oil, minced garlic, salt, and pepper in a separate bowl until well blended.

Add wet ingredients to the dry ingredients in the first bowl. Mix with a wooden spoon to form a batter. The batter should have a consistency of thin pudding.

Heat a deep skillet or Dutch oven over high heat until a few drops of water sprinkled on the surface sizzle and evaporate. Add vegetable oil and swirl to coat the pan. Ladle batter into skillet, filling it no more than halfway.

Working quickly, sprinkle the top with water and then add cornstarch. Repeat process of adding cornstarch and water in a circular pattern around the pan. The top should be covered with a very thin layer of cornstarch.

Cover pan with a lid and place in oven. Bake on the middle rack until a toothpick inserted in the center of the pan comes out clean.

Cool for 10 minutes, then invert the pan onto a rimmed baking sheet. Remove the pan and serve warm.

Garnish with minced garlic.

Assistant: The tire pressure sensor is the black tube mounted to the outside of the wheel.  Some tire pressure sensors use a solid piece of metal to sense the pressure, others use a spiral coil of thin wire wrapped around the tire sidewall.  A metal part of the sensor is connected to a circuit that sends a signal to the car's control system indicating the tire pressure.  The control system compares this signal with the pressure reading the car's onboard tire pressure sensor says is in the tire.  The tires may not be keeping up with the vehicle's actual tire pressure when the tires are cold, since they have some “elasticity”, so it's helpful to warm up the vehicle before checking the tires.

Assistant: Well first, I should tell you that the "shortcake" in strawberry shortcake is shortbread.  But it's not that hard! Here’s what you'll need:

1 cup (2 sticks) of butter
1 cup of granulated sugar
1 egg
1 teaspoon of vanilla extract
1 cup of flour
1⁄2 cup of sour cream
1 pint of strawberries, washed and sliced (You can use frozen berries if you prefer, just thaw them first)

Heat your oven to 325 degrees, and begin by combining the butter and sugar in a large bowl with an electric mixer.  Then beat in the egg and vanilla extract.  Next, add the flour and mix until a dough forms, which will be wet.  Then flatten the dough, cover, and chill in the fridge for half an hour.

Next, flour a clean surface, and roll out the dough to about 1⁄4-inch thickness.  Place the dough on a sheet pan, and bake for about 15 minutes, or until the edges are golden brown.  Leave the crust to cool while you whip up the strawberry filling.

For the filling, combine the sour cream and strawberries.  Then mash up the strawberries with a fork or a potato masher.  Finally, spread the berries over the crust, and garnish with extra berries and some powdered sugar.  Enjoy!

Ingredients:

-1/2 head of raw cabbage, shredded
-1/2 head of raw green cabbage, shredded
-1 cup chopped parsley
-1 pound chicken tripe
-1 pound chicken feet
-4 cloves garlic, peeled
-6 large onions, peeled
-2 tablespoons olive oil
-1 teaspoon cumin
-4 bay leaves
-Salt and pepper
-3 cups water
-1/4 cup vinegar
-1/4 cup extra-virgin olive oil

Instructions:

-Take out the veins and any other parts of the tripe that are hard.  This can be done with a clean, sharp knife, or with the tripe attachment on your food processor.

-Chop all of the ingredients into small pieces.  Then, add the olive oil to a large, wide saucepan and set it on medium-high heat.  Add the onion and garlic, and cook for 2 minutes, stirring occasionally, until they start to soften.  Add the parsley, cumin, and bay leaves.  Cook for a minute or two until they release their flavor into the dish.  Add the chopped vegetables and mix them together until they are well combined.

-Next, add the meat to the pot, and cook for another 5 minutes until the onion and garlic is soft and the tripe has released its fluid.  If you are working with chicken feet, remove the nails and the skin.  Then add the vinegar and water, cover the pot and let the soup simmer for 2-3 hours on low heat.  Add the vinegar gradually, and add water gradually, as needed, to keep the consistency of the soup.

-When the soup is ready, turn off the heat and let the soup cool.  Once it’s cool enough to handle, take the tripe out and shred it with your hands.  Now, add the shredded tripe to the pot of soup, and add the olive oil.  Using a hand blender, blend the soup until it’s smooth and creamy.  Add salt and pepper to taste

1. Take an 8×8 square piece of aluminum foil.  It will be the case for your pop-tarts.

2. Preheat the oven to 350 degrees.

3. Press 2 pieces of puff pastry together.  When you open the plastic wrapper, there’s a small piece of plastic that goes between the two pieces, so just throw that away.  Place the bottom pastry on the aluminum foil.

4. Press out all of the air bubbles that form in the dough.  Let the dough set for 5 minutes, and then use a knife to cut out circles.  Use a round pastry cutter to cut out circles of pastry for the top crust.

5. On the top of each circle of pastry, cut a small piece off and discard, so that you’re left with a disc.

6. Cover the top of each disc of pastry with either jam or preserves, depending on your preference.

7. Place the top crust on top of the pastry discs, and press gently to seal.

8. Place the tarts on a baking sheet, and bake for 10 to 15 minutes, depending on your oven and how long you need the tarts to cook.  They’ll be done when you see a nice browning on the top.

9. Cool for about 30 minutes.

10. Place an egg inside the center of each tart, and cover with the tarts’ top crusts.  Cook for 20 minutes.

11. Cool the pop-tarts for 10 minutes, and serve them on a platter.

Human: How are typhoons formed?

Assistant: They’re formed when the ocean heats up in a spiral pattern.  When the water temperature goes over 86°F, the ocean is too warm for there to be as many clouds or thunderstorms as there normally are.  So a lot of water evaporates and turns into a hot, rising layer of air, which cools down once it gets into the upper atmosphere.  That cooling process creates a lot of condensation, which forms clouds, which in turn create rain and snow.  And rain can cause runoff, which causes the ocean to rise and lower, which creates stronger winds and can lead to flooding.  When the winds grow strong enough, they can reach hurricane strength, even if the ocean temperature hasn’t increased all that much.  The hurricane then grows to a typhoon.

Most people probably have less than 100 mg of sodium per day, with the goal of intake being under 2,000 mg.  

Here are some of the health effects of over consumption of sodium:

-Too much sodium increases your risk of high blood pressure.

-When you have too much sodium in your diet, it can make you thirstier, so you drink more, which increases your risk of kidney stones.

-Excess sodium can make you retain water and be more bloated.

-Too much sodium can also make you put on weight, especially around your mid-section.

-The symptoms of too much sodium, like fatigue, nausea, dizziness, and muscle weakness can mimic the symptoms of low sodium intake.

Human: Hi, I'm making pumpkin pie for this Thanksgiving dinner, or I'm supposed to. Can you give me a good recipe so that I can do it well?

Assistant: Sure! Do you have access to pumpkin? If so, I would recommend this recipe. Here it is:

8 oz (226 g) pumpkin (approximately 1 medium to large squash)

4 eggs

1 1/2 cups (300 ml) light brown sugar

2 teaspoons (10 ml) pumpkin pie spice

1 1/2 teaspoons (7 ml) cinnamon

1/2 cup (120 ml) butter, melted

1 cup (240 ml) light corn syrup

1 cup (240 ml) evaporated milk

For the crust:

2 cups (500 ml) flour

1 1/2 teaspoons (7 ml) cinnamon

1/4 teaspoon (1 ml) salt

1 cup (240 ml) butter, chilled

1/3 cup (80 ml) shortening

1 teaspoon (5 ml) baking soda

3/4 teaspoon (4 ml) baking powder

1/4 cup (60 ml) chilled water

Preparation:

Preheat oven to 350°F (180°C, gas mark 4). Line a 9 x 11-inch (23 x 28 cm) baking pan with parchment paper.

Peel, seed, and dice pumpkin; set aside.

Make the crust: Mix together the flour, cinnamon, and salt. Cut in the butter and shortening until the mixture is crumbly.

Add the baking soda and baking powder. Mix in the water until it forms a dough.

Roll out the dough on a floured surface to form a 10 x 15-inch (25 x 38 cm) rectangle. Transfer the crust to the baking pan.

Top crust with the pumpkin, spreading it evenly to fill the pan.

Make the filling: Cream together the eggs, brown sugar, pumpkin pie spice, and cinnamon.

Mix in the butter and corn syrup.

Pour the mixture over the pumpkin in the crust.

Bake for 40 to 50 minutes, until the top is golden brown and the filling is set.

Cool for at least 10 minutes.

Assistant: There are many kinds of faucets, but in general faucets are either:

Manual (you turn the handle to let water flow), or

Automatic (you turn a valve somewhere, and the water just keeps coming out).

Each kind of faucet has its own unique failure modes.  For manual faucets, the basic ways that they can fail are:

They can be broken: they can lose their handle or the water just can’t turn off

They can be stuck open: they can be in the “open” position and the water just keeps coming out

They can be stuck closed: they can be in the “off” position and the water just won’t come out.

And, there are also some less common modes of failure:

They can get too hot and catch fire

The water pipe they’re attached to can get too hot and burst, and the water will just keep spraying out

For automatic faucets, there are several ways that they can fail:

They can just be broken.

They can be stuck open.

They can be stuck closed.

They can get too hot and catch fire.

Assistant: There are two main types of solar panels, monocrystalline silicon panels and polycrystalline silicon panels. In monocrystalline silicon panels, the silicon is grown in a single crystal form, so all of the layers have the same crystalline structure. This is important, because the different layers can be controlled very precisely to obtain the exact orientation of the atoms in the crystal. Because the layers have exactly the same crystal structure, all of the layers have the same conductivity and voltage. The top layer is used as the negative electrode, and the bottom layer is used as the positive electrode. The middle layer is called the intrinsic layer, because it contains the other layers and the ions that move within the cell. 

Polycrystalline silicon solar panels, on the other hand, are made from individual crystals grown in a random, non-controlled way. These crystals are then glued together. Because the crystals have non-uniform crystal structures, the layers have non-uniform conductivity and voltage. This means that the top layer is used as the negative electrode, the middle layer is used as the positive electrode, and the bottom layer is used as a diode.

chosen:
Human: How do whistles work?

Assistant: Whistles work by converting vibrations in a gas to sound.  When you blow a whistle, air from your lungs moves through a hole in the whistle (called the mouthpiece), creating a gas flow that is moving across the outside of the whistle, while the hole is closed.  Then, when you exhale (while blowing), some of that gas flows out of the hole, as the pressure of the gas in the whistle is lower than the air outside.  The sudden loss of gas pushes air out of the way, generating a column of air, and the loss of energy from the expanding gas causes sound waves to be generated.  This motion of air, coupled with air pushed aside by the expanding gas, is what we hear as the sound.

Human: Why does meat dry out in the freezer after a while?

Assistant: Well that’s a tricky one, humans! It turns out that you don’t actually freeze the meat, you only cool it down.  Meat still contains proteins and enzymes, and the proteins and enzymes aren’t affected by temperature, but they are affected by “crystallization” – when the cold liquid water in the meat becomes a solid.  It’s this crystallization that causes the meat to dry out.  On the other hand, if you freeze the meat, you freeze all the liquid, which prevents the crystallization from taking place, and the meat never dries out.  If you want to freeze meat without it drying out, you can put it in the fridge before you freeze it.

Sole Meuniere

The dish name literally means “miller’s wife,” and is usually made with a fresh Dover sole. Use fillets or Dover sole steaks.

Butter or clarified butter
Flour for dredging
Salt
Freshly ground black pepper
3 tablespoons fresh lemon juice
6 large whole Dover sole fillets or Dover sole steaks
Milk, if needed
4 shallots, finely diced
1/4 cup finely diced fresh flat-leaf parsley
8 parsley sprigs for garnish

Preheat a large, heavy nonstick frying pan over medium-high heat until very hot. Meanwhile, cut 3 tablespoons butter or clarified butter into 1/2-inch pieces and set aside. Sprinkle the flour on a dinner plate and season with salt and pepper.

Hold a fish fillet by the tail and dredge it first in the flour, shaking off any excess, then in the butter, shaking off any excess. Put in the pan and cook until golden brown, about 3 minutes per side. Drain on a platter or rack set over a sheet pan. Repeat with the remaining fish, butter, and salt and pepper, adjusting the heat as needed. If you do not have a large enough pan to cook the fish in one layer, cook the fillets, uncovered, for the first 3 minutes, then cover the pan and continue cooking. Add enough milk to the pan to keep the fish from sticking to the pan. 
-Add 1 tablespoon of the butter or clarified butter, the shallots, and 1/2 teaspoon of salt to the frying pan. Cook over medium-high heat until the shallots are soft and fragrant, about 1 minute. There’s a kind of version of Fricasé called a Pachamanca that’s served at traditional celebrations. That one contains pig’s feet, and is cooked in a fire pit in the ground, but other recipes don’t require those ingredients.

Here’s the recipe:

[image]

Pope Fricasé

1/2 lb. of kidney beans (if you can’t find kidney beans, any small beans will work)

5 cloves garlic

1 small white onion

2 tbsp. fresh ginger, peeled and chopped

1/2 tsp. salt

1 cup dry red wine

1 tbsp. ground cumin

1 tbsp. ground coriander

1/4 tsp. red chili powder

2 tsp. vegetable oil

1 tbsp. olive oil

1 1/2 lb. of lean pork or beef, cut into small cubes

Cook the beans with the cloves of garlic, onion, ginger, salt, red wine and 1 tablespoon of each of the spices for about half an hour, until the beans are soft.

Meanwhile, heat a large pan over medium heat and add the oils, then add the cubed pork or beef, browning it until all sides are brown, then add the spices and cook until the pork is cooked through.

Serve the pork on top of the beans and any leftover cooking liquid.

1 cup water

1/2 cup good wine, such as Pinot Noir

1/2 cup veal or beef broth, or a mixture

2 bay leaves

1 garlic clove, sliced in half

1 Tbsp. salt

Put on medium heat, bring to a boil, and then reduce heat to a simmer.  Let sit for an hour, and then strain through a fine mesh strainer into a small bowl.

Escargot sauce:

Put in a blender:

2 oz. bacon, cut in 1⁄4" pieces

2 tbsp. shallots, chopped

1 garlic clove, cut in half

1 cup water

1 cup beef broth

2 bay leaves

1 Tbsp. chopped parsley

3 tbsp. flour

1 tsp. salt

1/2 tsp. white pepper

1/2 tsp. dried thyme

1/2 tsp. dried rosemary

4 tbsp. butter

Blend on medium-high, and then transfer into a small saucepan.

Add the strained stock to the saucepan, and then bring to a boil over medium-high heat.  Add the salt, pepper, thyme, and rosemary.  Simmer for 25 minutes, stirring occasionally.

The sauce should be fairly thick; if it’s too thin, simmer and stir until it gets to the right consistency.  Turn off the heat and stir in the butter.

Recipe Type: Appetizer
Servings: 4
Prep time: 10 min
Cook time: 20 min

Crevettes grises (Gray shrimp) is a traditional Belgian appetizer. It’s a simple dish, but has a rich taste and very fresh shrimp.  
Ingredients:  
8 oz small shrimp (preferably from the north of the country, where they are smallest), shelled  
1.5 oz clarified butter  
1.5 oz flour  
2 cloves garlic, mashed with 1/2 oz breadcrumbs  
1 oz ham, shredded  
1/4 cup dry white wine  
1 1/2 oz low fat natural yogurt  
2 sprigs parsley, finely chopped  
2 oz water  
2 oz breadcrumbs  
2 oz butter, melted  
2 oz flour  
4 eggs, beaten

Method:  
Wash the shrimp and remove the heads, shells and tails.  
Rinse them, and dry.  
Mix the shrimp in a bowl with the ham, and the mashed garlic with breadcrumbs.  
Add the white wine.  
Let it marinade for 5 minutes.  
Heat up the clarified butter and put the flour in it.  
Keep stirring constantly, until the flour is absorbed and you get a paste.  
Put the marinated shrimp into the paste, and stir very well.  
In a skillet, add the yogurt and parsley and the water.  
Bring it to a boil and remove from the stove.  
Put the shrimp mixture on a plate.  
Take a spoon and make holes in it, filling each hole with the shrimp mixture.  
Put a small amount of water in a shallow bowl.  
Dip the shrimp into the water and then in the beaten egg.  
Put the shrimp on a lightly oiled baking sheet, and put the breadcrumbs and melted butter on top.  
Cook in a 350-degree oven for 20 minutes.  
The ideal way to serve it is to make shrimp balls with a teaspoon.  
These Crevettes grises are really good served with a cream sauce.

4 garlic cloves, minced

1 tsp. salt

1⁄4 tsp. white pepper

3.  Return the wok to medium-high heat. Add the eggs and ground pork mixture and stir-fry. Add the green beans and peas and stir-fry for about 2 minutes, then add the cornstarch and stir-fry for another 2 minutes, until the mixture is cooked through and looks glossy.
4.  Serve over rice and garnish with spring onions if desired.

It is not unusual for a hen to live the majority of her life in a cage, and sometimes she lives in that cage for her entire life. 1/2 teaspoon salt
Directions:

Aluminum oxide is also found in lava rock and geothermal bauxite deposits. By reacting bauxite with coke in a special electric arc furnace, aluminum oxide is extracted from bauxite. It is then mixed with aluminum metal (typically an alloy of aluminum) and melted together. The product of the reaction is dissolved in sulfuric acid. Then the water is evaporated, and the alumina is separated from the impurities by washing with water. The purified alumina is calcined in a kiln. Human: Can you give me a recipe for angel food cake?

Assistant: Sure, here’s one recipe:

Ingredients

1 cup cake flour
1 teaspoon salt
1⁄2 teaspoon baking powder
3 tablespoons white vinegar
3⁄4 cup sugar
4 large egg whites, at room temperature
4 large egg yolks, at room temperature
1⁄2 cup solid vegetable shortening
2 cups (1 pint) fresh berries (mix berries and their juice)
Whipped topping (optional)

Steps

Preheat oven to 325° F (163° C) and prepare an angel food pan.  Cream together the sugar and shortening until smooth.  Add eggs, one at a time, beating well after each addition.  Slowly add the dry ingredients.  Beat until fluffy.  Fold in the berries (and berry juice) by hand.

Pour the batter into the prepared pan, then bake for 60 to 75 minutes or until set.  Cool, then refrigerate and serve with whipped topping.  Refrigerate leftovers, but bring them to room temperature before serving.

Ingredients: 
 1 whole chicken breast
 1 tablespoon chicken base
 3 cups of chicken broth
 2 tomatoes, diced
 1/2 onion, diced
 3/4 cup coconut milk (light or full fat)
 Salt to taste

Instructions:


1.    Chop the chicken into bite-sized pieces and put it into a large stockpot.


2.    Add the chicken base and the chicken broth, and bring it to a boil.


3.    While the stock is coming to a boil, dice the tomatoes.


4.    Once the stock has reached a rolling boil, add the diced tomatoes and the diced onion.  This mixture will make the “tomato stock” for the soup.


5.    Once the tomato stock is boiling, add the coconut milk.  The coconut milk will now be mixed in, but don’t worry, this is good!  There is still more to add!



6.    Add the diced chicken and stir gently so that all the chicken is submerged.  Once the chicken is fully submerged, it is done!


To make this into soto ayam, simply make a clear soup.  Once it has reached a boil, add about 1/2 to 3/4 teaspoon of salt.  This will give it a sweet and sour taste, which is typical.  Add a bit more if you want to really make the soup taste salty, but I personally like to avoid this.  Garnish with sliced scallions and grated coconut.

Human: What is osteoarthritis?

Assistant: Osteoarthritis (OA) is a painful condition of the joints, especially the joints of the hands, wrists, knees, hips, feet, and spine. It is caused by a breakdown of the cartilage, or the tissue that cushions the ends of the bones. Osteoarthritis may be triggered by the wear and tear of the normal aging process, and may be worsened by physical activity, obesity, or injury. The pain of OA generally increases with activity, and may increase during the night. OA is classified as primary (or idiopathic) when there is no known cause for the osteoarthritis, and secondary when it is caused by another disease, such as rheumatoid arthritis. Symptoms of OA include swelling, a grating sensation, and the feeling of looseness. Severe pain, sometimes with bone erosion, may occur if the joint is displaced. Osteoarthritis may also cause a sensation of stiffness, and it may be associated with inflammation of the cartilage. While OA generally does not worsen, it does not cause further improvement in mobility or strength. Each time someone makes a payment from one address to another, the computer needs to make sure that they have the right to make the payment.  This requires checking that the sender has enough bitcoins in their wallet to make the payment, checking that the receiver has enough bitcoins in their wallet to receive it, and checking that they’re both paying the correct amount, and in the right direction, based on the addresses they’re using.  
If all that checks out, and a payment is allowed, then the receiver receives bitcoins into their wallet.  Then the sender’s wallet subtracts the amount paid.  The pools of miners collect a percentage of the fees paid by users of their pool, for processing each transaction.

1 1/2 cups milk

ice cubes, in trays, or a tray on a freezer

4 - 5 cups heavy cream

1/2 cup sugar

1/2 cup non-dairy substitute (such as soy or rice)

Prepare your freezing tray or bowl for your ice cream by pouring in the milk, cream, and sugar.

Heat your heavy cream, sugar, and non-dairy substitute on low until simmering.

Strain your mixture over the top of the cream, stir and mix well. 

Then take the ice cream base and put it into a freezer bowl.

Let it freeze for at least 8 hours or overnight.

Once the ice cream is frozen you can add whatever you want to it, like fruit. You can even put chocolate chips or cookie crumbs in it for a little added crunch.
